data.title: These Stocks Are The High-Yield Dividend Investor's Best Friend
data.description:
Have you ever asked yourself what type of high-yield stock would be your dream investment? This is important to ask yourself before selecting stocks because it sets the bar by which you assess every opportunity out there. This is especially important for high-yield investors because most high-yield stocks have some glaring flaws, hence why the market prices them at such high yields.

In many cases, it is because there are some serious question marks about the durability of the underlying business model. For example, Altria (MO) is priced at a sky-high yield because its core smokables business is declining sharply. Or in the case of Pfizer (PFE), it is also struggling with growth, and it has threats coming from expiring patent protections and growing generic brand competition, while it is counting heavily on its development pipeline to reignite its growth engine.

Other high yields are simply because they have a heavily over-leveraged balance sheet, which causes significant question marks about their ability to sustain the payout. A recent example of this is the late NextEra Energy Partners (XIFR) (now XPLR Infrastructure), which had to outright eliminate its distribution after years of growing at a brisk pace because of its significantly overextended balance sheet.

Still others have high yields because they have extremely low growth potential. A classic example of this is BDCs like Ares Capital (ARCC), which are required to pay out virtually all of their taxable income as dividends and therefore tend to generate relatively low growth over time unless they are able to issue shares at premiums to NAV and potentially retain some of their excess earnings and reinvest them at accretive enough returns to be able to offset headwinds from some non-accruals.

However, what if I told you that there was such a thing as a perfect high-yield stock, and in fact, there is a sector that is filled with them? In this article, I detail what sector of the market is currently the high-yield investor's best friend and also offer some of my top picks in that sector right now.

My Favorite High-Yield Sector Right Now

This sector is the infrastructure space (UTF), and in particular, energy infrastructure (AMLP). This is because it checks all my boxes. Namely, the business models are durable and, in fact, are well positioned to benefit from a major macro tailwind, which is the AI boom, especially in the United States. They are also quite defensive in nature, since energy infrastructure tends to be highly contracted on a long-term basis to creditworthy counterparties, or even in some cases, regulated, which makes it utility-like in nature.

On top of that, many of these energy infrastructure companies have very strong investment-grade balance sheets with low leverage ratios and well-laddered debt maturities. Additionally, many of these energy infrastructure businesses pay out very high yields, with some even reaching into the double digits. Yet, despite paying out high yields, they also generate solid and consistent growth rates that are positioned to be inflation-beating for years to come through a combination of contractual escalators, as well as sufficient retained capital that is being invested in high-returning organic growth opportunities that deliver very attractive risk-adjusted returns.

Not only that, but these businesses also tend to have conservative coverage ratios of their distribution, so even if their growth reverses, and they suffer a slight decline in cash flows over a short period of time, their distributions should not only be well-covered but likely will continue growing. This was most recently evidenced during the COVID crash in 2020, when many energy infrastructure businesses either saw a slight decline in cash flows or saw their cash flows stagnate, yet many continued growing their payouts right through that period of time.

Last but not least, they offer very attractive total return potential by combining their mid-to-high single-digit, or even in some cases double-digit, yields with attractive growth rates that range between 3% and 10% per year and have the potential for further valuation multiple expansion based on their own histories, as well as competing dividend growth instruments. When you put this all together, there is really not much to dislike, as investors looking for a reduction in sequence of return risk can get bond-like yields from this sector, yet get growth rates that make those bonds grow at rates that offset the corrosive power of inflation. Additionally, between their strong balance sheets and defensively positioned business models, the risk factor is not that high at all, especially if you diversify intelligently and insist on high-quality management teams.

Some Of My Top Picks Of The Moment

So, what are some specific opportunities that fit these criteria? Here are four that, I think, are particularly compelling at the moment. First, Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) is perhaps the best overall opportunity. It has a distribution yield of around 7%, it has a well-diversified business model across multiple different sub-segments within the midstream infrastructure space, and has a fully integrated business model. It also generates virtually all of its cash flows from long-duration contracts, giving it very minimal commodity price volatility. It has lengthy terms in those contracts, as well as inflation protections, and has the best credit rating in the entire midstream infrastructure space, with an A- credit rating from S&P. This is backed by a very low 3.1 times leverage ratio, significant liquidity, and about an 18-year weighted average term to maturity on its debt.

It also is positioned to benefit some from the AI boom due to its natural gas exposure and the location of its assets that could potentially benefit from data center demand. On top of all of that, it is likely going to grow its distribution at a 4% or higher CAGR for the foreseeable future, as it has numerous growth projects coming online in the next few years, which should give it significant additional capital allocation capacity, which management has hinted will likely go towards significant unit repurchases, along with other potential uses including special distributions or other growth opportunities.

Additionally, its distribution is very durable as it has a 1.7 times distributable cash flow coverage ratio. When combined with its roughly 7% yield, mid-single-digit expected growth outlook, and an EBITDA multiple that is below its historic averages, EPD appears poised to deliver very attractive mid-teens annualized total returns with minimal long-term downside risk.

Another two that I really like are Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP) and Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP). BIP is a diversified infrastructure play that also has midstream infrastructure assets as well as utilities, transportation, and data infrastructure assets. It offers a distribution yield of about 5% to go along with an expected AFFO per unit CAGR of 10%+ for the foreseeable future.

Additionally, it has a BBB+ credit rating, a conservative payout ratio of under 70%, and a highly contracted cash flow profile that also has the vast majority of its revenue coming from inflation-indexed contracts that make it both durable, defensive, and inflation resistant for years to come. When you combine the growth rate with the yield, as well as a priced AFFO multiple that is well below its historic averages, BIP appears poised to deliver mid-to-high teens annualized total returns with a fairly low-risk profile over the long term.

BEP is similarly positioned, though it focuses on clean energy infrastructure, with its largest single segment being hydropower but also having exposure to solar, wind, nuclear, battery, and other forms of renewable power. On top of that, its yield is higher at over 6%, and it boasts a similar growth profile—about a 10% AFFO per unit CAGR for the foreseeable future—as well as considerable upside relative to its historic priced AFFO valuation multiple. It also has a BBB+ credit rating and a highly contracted, inflation-resistant cash flow profile.

Finally, Enbridge (ENB) is an opportunity that gives investors exposure to a C-corporation in contrast to EPD, which is an MLP that issues a K-1 tax form. Albeit, BIP and BEP both have economic equivalent C-corporations in Brookfield Infrastructure Corporation (BIPC) and Brookfield Renewable Corporation (BEPC) that also give investors 1099 exposure. However, ENB is more of an energy infrastructure pure play, as it focuses on a combination of midstream and regulated utility assets and also is positioned to benefit from data center demand while offering a next twelve-month dividend yield of 6% that is expected to grow at an inflation-beating pace for the foreseeable future.

Additionally, nearly all of its cash flow is regulated, with the remainder being contracted, and virtually all of its counterparties are investment-grade, making it effectively a regulated utility that offers a higher yield than many other utilities out there. It also offers inflation-beating, meeting, or beating dividend growth for years to come without having to deal with a K-1 tax form—all while backed by a BBB+ credit rating as well.

Investor Takeaway

While many high-yield stocks are flawed and no investment is risk-free, the energy infrastructure space remains the high-yield investor's best friend since it offers high yields that are backed by strong balance sheets, very stable, highly contracted, and regulated cash flow profiles, and inflation-beating growth potential along with conservative payout ratios. Yes, there are risks such as short-term equity price volatility due to interest rate and energy price fluctuations, and as always, there is recontracting risk over the long term. However, for a patient long-term investor who diversifies intelligently, the short-term risks mean very little, and even the recontracting risks are minimal when investing in high-quality management teams with well-diversified asset portfolios and strong balance sheets. As such, infrastructure is by far the largest single sector in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: PLTY Vs. PLTW: Palantir Income 2 Ways
data.description:
Introduction

I've been vocal about my buy and strong buy ratings on Palantir (PLTR) stock since I wrote my first article on the company back in June 2024, although friends of mine have heard me geek out about Palantir long before then, too. I bought my first shares of the stock days after its initial offering, a "DPO" (because everything they do has to be both zany and "low-key"). That was back in December 2020, and it's been a wild ride since then.

I'm going to show a lot of "total return" charts. Note that the total return from YCharts is calculated including dividends paid, but not reinvested.

The stock is laden with risk, not only because it is currently overvalued by all valuation logic (my thesis is that sometimes, the market doesn't do the logical thing), but because its past drawdowns were horrendous. Top in 2021 to the bottom in 2023 was nearly -85%. It was not a good time to be a shareholder.

I've got more recent coverage of Palantir, if you want to learn more about their actual business, and the reasons I'm bullish, check out this article from back in February.

This is not a stock for the faint of heart, and yet of course in 2025, we have covered call funds on it.

Palantir In Covered Call Form

Today, I am not necessarily discussing Palantir, but "PLTR." What I mean by that, is that I am approaching PLTR as an asset to own just like one does the S&P 500 in a covered call strategy like NEOS' SPYI or Roundhill's XDTE.

This is also the stance of the two funds I'm reviewing in this article:

  • YieldMax PLTR Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:PLTY)
  • Roundhill PLTR WeeklyPay ETF (BATS:PLTW)

The goal of both funds is simple: own Palantir stock and sell the upside for income via the covered call strategy.

The covered call strategy is straightforward; own stock and sell calls against that stock position. This generates options premium, which is distributable as income to shareholders, but caps the upside on the stock position, meaning that the fund will stop gaining value on its stock position at some point.

The profit and loss chart of a covered call looks like this:

This is not a bad strategy, per se, as it can generate consistent income for shareholders without requiring them to time the selling of shares to produce income. They come with a price, however, as these funds often fail to keep up with their long-stock-only reference assets over time, due to that capped upside coming up at inopportune times. If it happens enough, the disparities start to add up.

Even in the very short time that PLTW has been on the market, we've already seen this in action, although props to PLTW for keeping up — although note the excess downside of the fund, something is going wrong there from a risk management standpoint if it's losing worse than the reference asset in these drawdowns.

This strategy is not incredibly well suited to investors looking for income, generally, as PLTR is not an asset that it too high volatility for me to recommend to most investors in the first place, let alone in a form that magnifies its downsides, such as PLTW seems to do (too short of timeframe to tell why, I explore this later), or caps its upside like PLTY.

If I'm going to speculate on Palantir, I want to just own the stock on this one, and I'm happy to leave my covered call exposure to an index like JEPQ or GPIQ, which sell covered calls on the Nasdaq 100 index, a high volatility stock index full of tech companies (and PLTR itself).

PLTY has a longer history, so we can pull the chart back further by excluding PLTW. The capped upside on this stock, which tends to run fast and hard to the upside when it does, hits its cap a lot on these covered call funds.

PLTY vs. PLTW Construction

Both funds are structured to own and sell covered calls against PLTR and benefit from the profit and loss of that trade.

PLTY

PLTY uses a synthetic covered call structure, which YieldMax employs on most of its funds. If you want a more in-depth guide to the YieldMax single-stock options strategy, I modeled it in this article about the Amazon (AMZN) variant, AMZY.

The short of it is that YieldMax holds treasuries in the ETF account and uses them to capitalize options that create the profit-and-loss of a covered call strategy, which is why PLTY's top ten holdings look like this:

PLTW

Meanwhile, PLTW has a trick up its sleeve — swaps. Total return swaps are essentially agreements that the participant's gains and losses on the product are the same as what would've happened on a real trade. Instead of taking the trade, they trade the swap. The downside is that swaps come with a cost.

So, why would they do that?

One, because whoever is selling them the swap is getting paid for it, and that's just good business for PLTW's managers, Roundhill Investments because they can guarantee the execution of the strategy. It also allows them to be sneaky and leverage up their position with a 100% allocation to the swap and extra Palantir stock.

That's why PLTW's holdings look like this:

Now, I'm on record for saying that leveraged Palantir is too bullish for me. I still believe that, and I can see a fund for what it's doing; trying to make up for the lack of upside.

This can be problematic as now we've come to a situation where instead of 100% downside, 80% upside (just an example of a generic covered call fund), we're at 120% downside, 100% upside.

If you owned PLTR stock outright, you'd have a 100% upside, too. But unlike PLTW, you wouldn't have a 120% downside.

That's why their charts look like this, and its low on the chart is significantly deeper than PLTR stocks.

The claim from Roundhill that it offers 120% PLTR's return is hardly true over its tenure so far, something I find common among leveraged funds in general.

This sets off a red flag for me, generally, and I caution income investors that gravitate toward these funds. When these funds are thrashing at their lows (which PLTR will likely do again in the future), it's very hard to want to keep this fund in your portfolio while it's underperforming unhedged stock on the downside by a significant amount.

Income investors are not typically well suited to these kinds of leveraged vehicles. They introduce too much volatility for not enough gain, generally speaking. We can see that in PLTW's chart.

None of this is to say that Roundhill doesn't make that clear, for the record. It's plastered all over the marketing for the fund.

However, the fund has failed to surpass PLTR's returns. I don't know how they are determining the leverage or executing it, as the fund is a black box inside the swap. Their website claims that they are not selling options, but I think that's just a technicality — they don't sell options, but they do receive the return of an options-selling strategy via the total return swap. We can see in its return pattern that it has the same upside and downside patterns of distributing funds that sell covered calls, only it also benefits (at least in its lifetime) from its leveraged long position, so it's kept up with PLTR's returns in total return, but not risk-adjusted returns.

This may have gotten a little long-winded. Here are the primary differences as I see it between these two funds:

YieldMax PLTY Roundhill PLTW
Long Method Synthetic Options Swaps & Long Stock
Short Method Options Swaps
Actively Managed Yes, Daily Yes, Weekly
PLTR Upside 80% 100%
PLTR Downside 100% 120%

PLTY vs. PLTW Income

It should be of note that this is the primary reason why a lot of income investors are interested in these funds, and they shouldn't be. These kinds of numbers are ridiculous and lure in folks who are unprepared to take the risks (remember, -85% over 2 years grinding downwards) that it comes with.

PLTY advertises a distribution rate of 140%, although this comes off the back of a massive distribution that may not be repeated anytime soon. Like all YieldMax single-stock funds, it distributes monthly.

PLTW's distribution rate of nearly 54% seems paltry in comparison, but it is still massive. As the name implies, the fund pays out weekly to shareholders and has been hard at work generating income since its inception, but it follows the same trend as PLTY's.

These funds both distribute "return-of-capital" in their distributions, which come with a unique tax situation many investors find advantageous for taxable brokerage accounts. If you want to learn more, I go into depth on the subject in this recent article on NEOS' QQQI.

Suitability

Just as I feel about basically all single-stock covered call funds, they fail to live up to the hype for me. They tend to be too volatile, reliant on idiosyncratic movements in an underlying stock, to be suitable for most income investors, and those who are brave enough to take on the risk would rather take the upside of just owning long stock and be compensated for that risk.

I do think the strategies have potential, especially the YieldMax single-stock funds, but only in aggregate, using diversity to mitigate those idiosyncratic stock risks that make the funds unsuitable in most doses on their own.

To that end, I still think investors interested in this fund are better off owning it alongside a bunch of the other funds, like with YMAX, to spread the risk. If you want to own Palantir, you're better served just owning the stock itself. I don't want to cap its upside because I am very bullish on the stock, but I also don't want to take on leverage — I could better do that myself with call options and LEAPS than with PLTW because I wouldn't have the income component to deal with.

At the end of the day, I believe neither PLTY nor PLTW are very suitable for income investors. Speculators could seek a 1% position at the max in either of these names.

Conclusion

Ultimately, I still don't understand why folks want to cap Palantir's upside through the use of covered calls, and I believe that investors in the stock are better off just holding long stock in most cases.

To that extent, I reviewed two funds:

  • YieldMax PLTR Option Income Strategy ETF
  • Roundhill PLTR WeeklyPay ETF

Both funds come with their intricacies and complications, and I see faults in both funds as they relate to holding a Palantir position. Investors are advised caution regarding these funds. Palantir is a stock that does not lend itself well to the covered call strategy as I see it. It carries too much risk to make the income worth it because it ultimately means you'll miss out on Palantir when it's at its best, which often comes after earnings reports and contract announcements, when these funds may end up not seeing all of that upside due to the caps placed on it from the covered call strategy.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Guidewire Software Is Soaring, But I'm Not Biting
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Guidewire Software (NYSE:GWRE), the property and casualty insurer, is an underfollowed name. But that has not been an impediment to its stock more than doubling in the past 12 months.

What's more, with the stock jumping higher after its fiscal Q3 2025 earnings and solid guidance ahead, the stock appears unstoppable.

Moreover, they are raising their full-year outlook and expect to grow ARR in the high teens percentage-wise, which is strong.

However, as I appraise this name, I struggle to get overly comfortable with paying 67x forward free cash flow for this business.

So, I'll stay neutral and wish the bulls good luck.

Guidewire Software's Near-Term Prospects

Guidewire makes software that helps insurance companies run better.

It's a large, high-tech platform that handles everything from processing claims to launching new insurance products. Their main selling point is Guidewire Cloud, a platform that's flexible and makes life way simpler for insurers compared to the old, legacy systems they used to rely on.

For insurers, that means a better customer experience and the ability to adapt quickly when things change, like rolling out a new product in days instead of months.

And yet, Guidewire still faces the challenge of getting some slower-moving insurers to ditch their old systems and commit to big cloud transformations.

Also, even though this business is now nearly 25 years old, they are showing investors that they still have what it takes to be innovative and deploy products at scale, particularly as AI becomes more of a factor in the insurance world.

Given that balanced background, let's now discuss its fundamentals.

Fiscal 2026 Could Grow at 14% to 17% Y/Y

Guidewire Software delivered a solid revenue beat in what was its most challenging quarter of the year to beat.

Indeed, looking back, Guidewire has rarely failed to deliver a beat against analysts' expectations.

With this year practically concluded, the challenge now is to come up with some sort of revenue growth rate estimate for next fiscal year, which starts in August 2025.

On the one hand, the business is pointing towards high teens of ARR, see the quote from the earnings call to support this:

But kind of getting into that maintaining upper teens fully ramped ARR growth would be a tremendous outcome for us. To do that three years in a row, and that's the direction we're working towards.

On the other hand, the business does have very high and challenging comparable quarters to be up against. So, I believe that a lot needs to go right for the business to smash through the mid-teens analysts' expectations in fiscal 2026.

Basically, we are probably looking at around 14% to 17% y/y revenue growth for the year ahead.

What about its valuation? That's what we'll discuss now.

GWRE Stock Valuation—67x Forward Free Cash Flow

As an Inflection investor, I'm put off by Guidewire's balance. Not including its long-term investments, which could take some time to convert to cash, it has approximately $250 million in net cash. This means that about 1% of its market cap is made up of cash. As a reference point, I typically back companies with 5% net cash to market cap or higher.

Moving on, I estimate that Guidewire Software's free cash flow this year will be around $25 million, up slightly from the $18 million deployed in fiscal 2024.

If this estimate is roughly accurate, this will mean that its free cash flow this fiscal year could reach around $250 million.

This would be about 40% higher than the free cash flow reported in the prior year. However, since this year is practically completed, it makes more sense to turn our focus to fiscal 2026.

And assuming that its free cash flow continues to move up by a more moderate level, of say, 20% y/y, this could see Guidewire delivering $300 million of free cash flow.

This means that GWRE is priced at 67x forward free cash flow, a figure that I am not completely sold on.

Simply put, not only is the business delivering about mid-teen growth rates, but I'm also having to embrace a balance sheet that is weaker than I desire?

And on top of that, having to pay a hefty premium? That won't work for me, so I'll stay neutral on this name.

Risk Factors to the Upside

Investors love backing companies that are accelerating their prospects. Particularly when it comes on the back of a strong year already on the books.

This often leads the multiple to trade at what I find elevated valuations. The fact that the stock is richly priced very often has no bearing on where the stock can go in the medium term.

So, even though I believe that it doesn't quite make the cut for my Deep Value Returns portfolio, this doesn't necessarily mean that I'm making the right call to remain neutral on this stock.

On top of that, they just had one of their best sales quarters ever, with tons of new deals. Furthermore, their annual recurring revenue is pointing towards mid-teens growth, and pushing past $1 billion, which is a huge milestone that will attract institutions to hold the stock, thus making it less volatile and able to trade at a higher premium.

The Bottom Line

Look, I'll give credit where it's due—Guidewire is executing well.

Strong ARR growth, an improving top line, and a platform that's clearly resonating with insurers.

But when I zoom out and look at the financials, paying 67x forward free cash flow for mid-teen growth just doesn't sit right with me.

The balance sheet isn't as robust as I'd like, and the valuation leaves very little room for error.

I'm not calling a top—far from it—but for my style, this one's priced for perfection. So while I'm impressed, I'll stay on the sidelines for now and cheer from afar.

data.title: Rare Earths And The Trade War
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Critical minerals

It's not being publicly advertised, but access to rare earths is playing an outsized role in resurgent U.S.-China trade tensions. Following last month's détente in Geneva, it was apparently agreed that both sides would roll back retaliatory tariffs imposed since April 2, but there has been disagreement over export controls and sector-specific levies. China appears to be keeping in place a strict approvals process on rare earths that are used in everything from commercial vehicles to military equipment, while the U.S. has increased tariffs on steel and aluminum to 50%.

Quote: "I like President XI of China, always have, and always will, but he is VERY TOUGH, AND EXTREMELY HARD TO MAKE A DEAL WITH!!!" President Trump wrote early this morning on Truth Social. It comes just days after another post that highlighted the escalating tensions. "China, perhaps not surprisingly to some, HAS TOTALLY VIOLATED ITS AGREEMENT WITH US. So much for being Mr. NICE GUY!"

Carmakers have warned of dire consequences if things don't change quickly, with a trade group representing General Motors (GM), Toyota (TM) and other major producers recently sending a letter to the White House. "Without reliable access to these elements and magnets, automotive suppliers will be unable to produce critical automotive components," according to the Alliance for Automotive Innovation. On the military side, rare earth magnets are included in the production of fighter jets and aerospace components like missile guidance systems, impacting firms like Raytheon (RTX) and Lockheed Martin (LMT).

What are rare earths? Despite the name, there are deposits of them all over the world (some of them are even thousands of times more abundant than gold). The term refers to a group of 17 elements that are called "rare" since it is unusual to find them in pure form or in concentrated quantities. As a result, the minerals are difficult to mine and refine profitably, while byproducts like toxic wastewater and radioactive residues have to be addressed during extraction and processing.

Outlook: China undercut world prices for rare earths in the 1990s, resulting in heavy barriers to entry and putting pressure on upcoming challengers. Hopes of U.S. companies looking to break into the industry have also been dashed in the past due to strict environmental regulations, leading China to gain a 90% share of the world's refining capacity. It could take 5-10 years for any serious American production to get off the ground, and it's another reason why the Trump administration has made rare earths a prominent theme during negotiations with Ukraine and its play for Greenland.

Here's the latest Seeking Alpha analysis

My Top 2 Buy And Hold Forever BDC Picks

Nebius: Hyperscaler Aiming For Hypergrowth

Rare Buying Opportunities: 2 Top Picks Set To Soar

Who Else Likes High Yield And High Returns? (2 Beaten Down Buys)

Occidental Vs. Chevron: Buffett Bought Both, But Which Is Better For You?

What else is happening...

Nvidia (NVDA) reclaims crown as most valuable company.

Meta's nuclear power deal likely cheaper than Microsoft's.

Texas could soon mandate warning labels on Doritos.

Mastercard and PayPal team up on checkout flexibility.

AMC theaters to show more ads before playing movies.

Elon Musk bashes Trump's 'One Big, Beautiful Bill.'

SpaceX revenue forecast; Rocket Lab's (RKLB) next launch.

Chinese startup unveils text-to-video to take on OpenAI.

South Korea's opposition candidate wins presidential election.

NATO wants European members to ramp up air defenses.

Today's Markets

In Asia, Japan +0.8%. Hong Kong +0.6%. China +0.4%. India +0.3%.
In Europe, at midday, London +0.2%. Paris +0.7%. Frankfurt +0.7%.
Futures at 6:30, Dow +0.1%. S&P +0.1%. Nasdaq +0.1%. Crude +0.2% to $63.51. Gold +0.2% to $3,383.60. Bitcoin +0.3% to $105,735.
Ten-year Treasury Yield -1 bp to 4.46%.

On The Calendar

Companies reporting today include MongoDB (MDB) and ChargePoint (CHPT).

See the full earnings calendar on Seeking Alpha, as well as today's economic calendar.

data.title: Logitech: Product Innovation And Positive Underlying Demand To Drive Growth
data.description:
Investment Summary

My previous investment thought on Logitech (NASDAQ:LOGI) was a buy rating, as there were solid developments seen in the 3Q25 results, especially in the VC segment and rollout of AI-powered solutions. The underlying demand remains strong, and the growth momentum should continue with LOGI's focus on product innovation. I am less worried about the tariffs, as LOGI has a visible path to navigate them with pricing and supply chain agility. Overall. I maintain my buy rating.

Resilient Consumer Demand

The underlying profile remains positive, and there are multiple data points to support my view. First of all, the latest quarter (4Q25) showed that sell-through is outpacing sell-in, and this is the clearest sign of a positive demand trend (in 4Q25, management confirmed that sell-through exceeded sell-in by about 200bps). Viewed from another angle, this suggests a healthy pull-based demand (much more sustainable than push-based).

The second data point is drawn from Morgan Stanley’s May AlphaWise survey. The report showed a sharp improvement in both sentiment and spending intentions compared to April, particularly for electronics. While they are still in negative territory overall, the 6-month forward outlook for electronics spending jumped 17 points month-on-month to -8%, and this marks a near 4-year high. And perhaps an even more encouraging data point is that, for high-income households, the reading turned positive for the first time in several months, hitting 9% (note this is also the best result over the past three years).

Both these data points paint a very solid demand backdrop for LOGI, and given that LOGI still holds the leading market position in 11 of the 13 categories it competes in, I remain confident in the earnings growth outlook.

Strategic Initiatives and Product Innovation to Sustain Gaming Growth Momentum

Building on what I wrote about the gaming segment previously, I still think it is valid. LOGI ended FY25 with 10% constant currency revenue growth (9% reported), which is the best-performing segment across all other categories. While the gaming segment in 4Q25 saw a y/y decline, that was due to channel inventory normalization, not weak demand. I have two reasons to believe the gaming growth momentum will continue for the foreseeable future.

Firstly, the “China for China” continues to work. As a recap, this strategy was rolled out in early to mid-FY24, with the focus on launching products specifically for the Chinese market—both premium and entry-level—at a faster pace. On the 4Q25 call, management noted that they were “really pleased by the results.” A good example to showcase how successful this strategy is the Alto Keys Mechanical Customizable Keyboard, which became the best-selling personal workspace mechanical keyboard in China. The impressive thing is this is not even a gaming product, so it goes to show how effective this localized approach has been. Given that the strategy is apparently working, like I have pointed out before, this should continue to help LOGI capitalize on the so-called “lipstick effect” (consumers spending on affordable “luxury” like gaming).

Secondly, LOGI continues to be focused on high-profile partnerships. The launch of POWERPLAY 2, alongside high-profile collaborations with McLaren, shows that LOGI is very focused on winning the premium gaming segment. The McLaren tie-up is especially impactful, as it aligns Logitech G’s brand image with a top motorsport brand and also helps to solidify LOGI’s presence in the high-end simulation racing category.

AI Integration

LOGI AI push is gaining momentum, and I believe this has become an even more important growth catalyst. The previous partnership I highlighted in the 3Q25 update was just a simple NVIDIA streaming assistant, but the partnership has evolved to target broader product categories and internal workflows. We can already see some of these AI showing up in features like smart camera switching in meeting rooms, digital cocooning for video calls, and AI-powered noise cancellation in headsets. This is amazing from a user experience perspective, which I see as the most critical attribute that differentiates one product from another. There are just too many products in the market that can serve the same purpose as LOGI’s products. But if LOGI is able to leverage AI to differentiate itself, it is a big win.

More notably, it’s the internal benefit from the Logi IQ platform—a proprietary AI engine that helps LOGI to accelerate everything from product ideation to development using years of engineering data. This enhanced productivity could be the start of a super cycle of product innovation/rollout could potentially put LOGI in a much better competitive position vs. peers that lack proprietary engineering data, consumer preference data, and scale to run a massive R&D budget (as a percentage of sales, R&D stays well above 6%).

Pricing Power and Supply Chain Agility

Tariffs have been a huge focus and debate point since my last update, but I am not as worried as market participants might be. There are two points to note here.

One is that LOGI has pricing power, and it has exerted it to protect against tariff costs—GI raised US prices by 9–10% on average, and management noted little disruption after implementation. This, again, speaks well of LOGI's leading position and also shows that the market is willing to pay for quality.

Two, LOGI has been actively cutting its US-bound sourcing from China and has a target to reduce it from 40% to 10% by the end of CY25. This is possible because of LOGI's “China plus five” model—the supply chain relies on China and five other countries, including Vietnam, Mexico, Thailand, and Malaysia. And Taiwan, where its supply chain is structured in a way that allows production to shift quickly. As such, even though tariffs will weigh on near-term gross margins (this is inevitable as it takes time for LOGI to move its supply chain), I don’t see this as a structural margin hit. Over time, as LOGI continues to raise prices and diversify its supply chain, there shouldn’t be an issue for margins to recover.

Valuation

Comparing current consensus estimates to the last time I updated LOGI, it appears that the market has pushed out LOGI adj. EPS estimates by one year (the original FY27 EPS estimate is now for FY28). That is alright, and I think it's fair to be conservative in the current macro environment where tariff uncertainty makes it hard to forecast near-term demand—despite the data shown above that demand sentiment remains strong. For valuation purposes, I will stick to FY28 numbers, since it was what I used in February this year. The current estimate is ~$5.1, which, if adjusted for the historical track record of consensus underestimating LOGI’s EPS, the “effective” adj. EPS estimate should be close to ~$5.5. Reflecting the uncertain macro backdrop, I attached a 19x forward PE (1x discount to the historical average of 20x), and this implies a share price target of $104.5, which is ~27% upside.

Risk

In terms of tariffs, while LOGI has moved quickly to pass on costs through price hikes and diversify sourcing, there is no guarantee that demand will not fall off the cliff because of higher prices, especially in a weak macro environment. Any weakness in consumer sentiment—especially in the US—could cause growth to stall. Moreover, the push to reduce US-bound imports from China to just 10% by CY25 requires flawless execution. If there are hiccups, it could lead to LOGI missing this target and potentially cause operation disruption (like lack of inventory to sell).

Conclusion

My view for LOGI is a buy rating. There is visible evidence that demand remains strong, and the tariff situation does not look as bad as it seems. Even after adjusting for the push-out in adj. EPS estimates and a cheaper multiple assumption, I believe the upside is still attractive.

data.title: ZIM Integrated Shipping: 17% Yield Plus A Juicy Catalyst
data.description:
ZIM Integrated Shipping Services (NYSE:ZIM) is benefiting from improving fundamentals in the shipping industry, mainly high shipping prices. Although freight rates have seen a negative price trend this year, the shipping company is still well-positioned to benefit from growing demand for shipping services going forward. ZIM Integrated Shipping confirmed its outlook for FY 2025 EBITDA and also saw tailwinds from double-digit container volume growth in the last quarter. I like ZIM Integrated Shipping for its low valuation based on earnings and because all major key financial metrics have increased considerably in Q1'25. In my opinion, the investment setup is still very much favorable for the shipping company, and I recommend shares of ZIM Integrated Shipping as a strong buy.

Previous Rating

I rated shares of ZIM Integrated Shipping a strong buy in my last work on the shipping company due to a strong outlook for global shipping. ZIM benefited from strong growth in container shipments in the first-quarter and freight prices may have already reached their bottom in Q2'25, creating potentially potent tailwinds for revenue, EBITDA and free cash flow growth. With the U.S. and China working towards a trade compromise, the shipping industry may get set up for a massive recovery rally in the next several months if a final deal between the two largest economies in the world comes to fruition.

Freight Prices May Have Already Bottomed

Double-digit volume growth and higher average freight rates have been highly supportive of ZIM Integrated Shipping being able to post robust financial results for its first fiscal quarter.

ZIM Integrated Shipping generated $2.0B in revenue for Q1'25, showing 28% year-over-year growth which was driven by 1) A higher number of transported containers, and 2) Higher average shipping rates. In the first-quarter, ZIM Integrated Shipping benefited from a 12% surge in container volumes year-over-year while average freight rates surged 22% year-over-year to $1,776 per shipped container.

As a result, all major financial metrics in ZIM Integrated Shipping's business surged in Q1'25, including its adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow. EBITDA is a major financial metric for shipping companies, as it is a measure of operational performance unaffected by non-cash items such as depreciation or amortization.

In the first-quarter of FY 2025, ZIM Integrated Shipping generated $779M in EBITDA, showing 82% year-over-year growth as well as a 12 PP margin improvement to 39% year-over-year. Mainly higher container volumes and a significant recovery in freight rates have boosted the company's financial performance in Q1'25, and I believe the outlook for the shipping industry in generally is highly favorable.

According to the Drewry Container Index, freight prices increased to $2,508 per 40ft container, showing an increase of 10% over the previous week. Shipping prices appear to have bottomed in the first week of May, which is when it companies were billed an average of $2,076 per 40ft container shipment.

Since hitting this low, shipping prices have rebounded a massive 21% and the rebound is mainly related to improving investor sentiment following an announcement in May that the U.S. and China would temporarily lower their tariffs to 10%, for a period of 90 days. With shipping rates seemingly bottoming, a key catalyst for a continual price recovery could be a trade compromise between the U.S. and China, which is the main reason why freight rates sharply corrected to the downside throughout the first-quarter.

With an uptick in freight rates, year-over-year, ZIM Integrated Shipping also saw strong free cash flows. In the first-quarter, ZIM Integrated Shipping generated $787M in free cash flow, showing 160% year-over-year growth. The increased free cash flow was mainly used by the company to lower its net debt.

Improving free cash flow as well as improving EBITDA/net income picture strongly suggest that the shipping company will continue to pay shareholders a juicy quarterly dividend. As per the company's policy, it pays investors 30% of its quarterly net profits as dividends, which translates, based on Q1'25 profitability, to a 17% forward dividend yield. However, if ZIM Integrated Shipping were to see drastically declining shipping rates for the remainder of the year and the firm's net income dropped below zero, shareholders would receive no dividend at all.

ZIM's Outlook for FY 2025

ZIM Integrated Shipping confirmed its EBITDA outlook for FY 2025 and the shipping company continues to expect $1.6-2.2 billion in adjusted earnings before interest, taxes and appreciation this year. The main catalyst for higher adjusted EBITDA could come from a continual rebound in shipping rates following a potential trade compromise between the U.S. and China. Therefore, I believe the possibility of a trade conflict resolution and a normalization of trade relationships is the single biggest reason for investors to own shares.

ZIM Integrated Shipping's Valuation

Because of the drop-off in shipping rates between January and May, shipping companies like ZIM Integrated Shipping have seen renewed valuation pressure. However, with the U.S. and China striking more conciliatory tones lately and both countries agreeing to lower tariffs while working out a new trade deal, I believe shipping rates may have already seen their bottom.

As a result, a major share price revaluation may follow a continual recovery of shipping rates. ZIM Integrated Shipping is currently trading at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 4.9X, implying an earnings yield of 20% which seems excessive to me, especially with ZIM Integrated Shipping remaining widely profitable in Q1'25. Genco Shipping & Trading (GNK) is trading at a much higher P/E ratio of 36.6X, which implies an earnings yield of 3%.

In my opinion, ZIM Integrated Shipping could easily reprice to a ~10X P/E ratio, under the condition that the trade war between the U.S. and China gets resolved amicably, and that shipping companies face fewer earnings uncertainty. In such a case, it is also likely that clarity about trade conditions could lead to growing container shipments and higher fleet utilization for shipping companies. A 10X P/E ratio implies a fair value for ZIM Integrated Shipping of ~$34 per-share, meaning shares could easily double for their current price level.

Risks With ZIM Integrated Shipping

The biggest risk for ZIM Integrated Shipping is a (cyclical) down-turn in freight rates which would negatively impact all of the company's major financial metrics such as revenue, EBITDA and free cash flow. Lower shipping rates are therefore the single most serious risk factor that investors have to deal with. On the other hand, even during periods of massive freight rate contractions, ZIM Integrated Shipping continued to service its customers and returned after a short period of losses back to paying shareholders a healthy dividend.

Final Thoughts

ZIM Integrated Shipping is a well-run shipping logistics company that offers investors the opportunity to participate in the growth of the global shipping industry. ZIM Integrated Shipping is seeing double-digit container shipment growth, strong revenue tailwinds and with shipping rates potentially already seeing their bottom, a new price up-leg may await investors in the months ahead. ZIM Integrated Shipping is trading at an attractive valuation based on earnings and with the company confirming its EBITDA outlook for FY 2025, I believe ZIM could be a potentially attractive investment here, with theoretical upside of more than 100%. Additionally, since ZIM Integrated Shipping is profitable again, the shipping company is also on track to keep paying healthy quarterly dividends, which should boost investors' effective investment yield.

data.title: Alphabet: A Hold Your Nose Buy
data.description:
Premise: GOOGL Hasn't Lost Compounder Status Yet, but You're Going to Have to Hold Your Nose for a Few Quarters

We're long Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) seeing a low 20s upside from current levels by YE'26.

That said, we think the story for the next couple of quarters is going to be continued decelerations in paid click growth on Search, bringing the terminal value debate on the business to its head. That's just not priced at $170 / 18x fwd earnings.

We can see $150-$155/shr marking something like a bottom for this (16-16.5x '25 EPS of $9.45/14-15x '26 EPS of $10.50).

We think GOOGL ultimately wins this debate, and so it's a hold your nose long from here as we can underwrite ~20% 12-18M upside with $170 as the start point. That's definitely better than some of the other FAANGM names.

To be a GOOGL bull, you ultimately have to believe that ecosystem lock isn't real for AI models yet, and that engineering ultimately trumps design/first-mover advantage.

We think the jury's still out on that debate. They're obviously picking up speed over call it the last three months with Gemini 2.5, Firebase, and now Veo 3, so there's an extent to which these products are additive to GOOGL's mindshare.

We think the real bull case on GOOGL and AI rests in ecosystem integration. Raw performance is most important for enterprises (particularly w/customer service agents/coding tasks), but for consumers, we think it's going to be about bespokeness and lengthy context windows.

We think GOOGL will be able to create AI-powered experiences across its ecosystem that other model providers simply don't have access to. For one, GOOGL is the only leading model provider with a mobile operating system used by >1b people. We think integrated on-device experiences that's the logical conclusion of something like Project Mariner, and that there's the potential for GOOGL to further monetize their subscriptions business a-la AAPL's Services segment.

Taking a step back, our bull thesis on GOOGL is underpinned by: (a.) AI leadership across engineering/infra, (b.) de-risking from a Google Network spin-out, (c.) narrative optionality around 'getting fit'/mgmt. change down the road, (d.) call options w/Waymo+GCP, (e.) position to disintermediate enterprise software w/AI.

Backdrop & Our View

GOOGL has inarguably been the worst narrative laggard in Mag7 since the '22 rebound. It stands alone because it's the only name facing a terminal value debate. This is especially ironic considering, at the time, Google was considered the most difficult to disrupt business model in tech.

That's why the multiple has done this vs. the rest of mega-cap internet/software:

Since ChatGPT's launch at the end of 2022, there has been a narrative ebb-and-flow w/GOOGL.

Initially, there was doubt around the 'true' efficacy of LLM chatbots (positive for GOOGL as search won't be disrupted) --> Gemini launch disaster (negative for GOOGL as laggard perception is created while GPT has only improved/scaled adoption) --> alt-data/sell-side surveys suggesting that chatbots are eating Search queries (negative for GOOGL as engagement leads paid clicks leads revenue) --> last ~3 ish months of product launches/announcements from Gemini 2.5 to Firebase to now Veo 3 (positive GOOGL as they're AI winners starting to ship product).

All the while, this narrative whipsaw has led to multiple compression vs. peers.

We think the opportunity here is simple: EPS estimates are too low, and so are multiples assuming anything but the worst-case scenario. That said, you're not going to have fun owning this as it will take time and execution for the market to reward you for your patience.

Tactical View

If you had to summarize the reason for GOOGL's underperformance into one KPI, it's paid clicks.

To the extent that there is still a debate re: whether or not chatbots are eating commercial queries vs. task queries, we think that if it's not happening right now, it's bound to start happening over the next few quarters. This is inevitable as chatbots continue to eat traditional search queries and develop a greater presence in users' time spent.

You can make the argument that commercial queries haven't been impacted yet, but it's hard to say there's a 'real' barrier to this happening with how much models are improving and becoming more customized.

AI Leadership

The perception that GOOGL is an AI laggard is just wrong. We understand why people think this. The search model of the 1990s internet is archaic and AI is supplanting it.

That said, on technological/product leadership, we find it harder to believe that GOOGL is a laggard.

Gemini 2.5 Pro has industry-leading performance across reasoning, coding, and problem-solving. Veo 3 is a paradigm-shifting video product. This is born out in the performance tests. Top it off with GOOGL's scaled infrastructure and highly talented engineering teams, and you can put to bed that their tech is somehow behind.

So the question hasn't been whether or not they can build good tech. It's whether or not they can protect/replicate the Search cash cow.

While the jury's still out on whether or not this happens, I find it hard to believe that a 'recommendations' ad product layered into AI search would monetize at a structurally lower rate than the 10 blue links model.

That said, we have no problem believing that the transition from Search --> AI Mode for both engagement and advertisers might be a monetization hiccup similar to Meta's transition from Stories --> Reels a few years back where impressions growth slowed as the format scaled and ROAS was lower than Feed.

Google Network Spin-Out

Plenty of ink has been spilled as to the upsides/downsides of a GOOGL breakup. While we don't particularly buy the sum-of-the-parts bull case on divesting segments, we think there is something to be said about a potential divestiture of GOOGL's ad-buying and selling arms.

These businesses are (a.) seeing revenue shrink Y/Y and (b.) negatively impact the overall ad business' margin mix.

More importantly, you remove the tail risk of regulation if Judge Brinkema rules that Google Network has to be divested as investors pencil in a smaller (by revenue) GOOGL that produces higher margins.

Narrative Optionality

When game-theorying out GOOGL's next six-twelve months, the reason we feel compelled to be long now is simple. The narrative is largely still in management's hands.

Are paid clicks going to slow? Yes. Is there a scenario where Search is growing revenue low to mid single digits? Yes. Is that scenario plausible as the AI model you're using today is the worst one you're ever going to use and the value gap between traditional search and 'answer engines' continues to accelerate? Yes.

So what do I mean by narrative optionality?

I mean that there are still levers where, in the scenario where Street revenue numbers have to get cut, EPS numbers can still go up.

I think the setup on GOOGL is very similar to the setup on META in 2022. We're entering a period where exogenous factors hit the financials (TikTok/IDFA in META's case), and the terminal value debate reaches a fever pitch.

It's worth remembering that as META developed Reels as a competitor to TikTok, it took time to scale engagement, and even more time to close the monetization gap.

However, the real lever management pulled when growth hit a wall was layoffs.

The closest thing GOOGL did to mass-layoffs was cut 6% of their global workforce in 2023.

In a scenario where Search growth is materially slower, a 10-15% reduction in headcount could provide the cushion necessary for EPS to backstop the stock as management figures out how to management the revenue transition on Search.

Risks and Conclusion

We don't see GOOGL as a top idea on the internet right now. The left-tail risks around a Chrome divestiture, along with the aforementioned paid clicks deceleration and a not 'dirt cheap' valuation, leave us relatively cautious.

That's why we're not invested at a full position until we see some of these downside catalysts play out.

However, management has a lot of leverage with regard to the cost structure of the business, so we think even in the event of revenue growth slowing, EPS estimates are at the very least defensible.

data.title: Prospect Capital: Out-Of-Touch Valuation
data.description:
My last stock classification Prospect Capital Corp. (NASDAQ:PSEC) was 'Buy' as the business development company suffered some issues related to a decrease in payment-in-kind interest.

The BDC, however, cut down its dividend which has led to a higher margin of safety for passive income investors and the business development company maintained a high degree of credit quality, given its low non-accrual ratio of 0.6% in 1Q25. A high degree of insider ownership is also something that I think backs a bullish stock classification for Prospect Capital.

Portfolio Shifting Toward First Liens, Origination and Income Trends

Prospect Capital famously pivoted away from collateralized loan obligations and real estate investments in 2024 in favor of its First Lien debt origination business. Passive income investors have already seen a minor change in the business development companies portfolio mix as a consequence: About 65.5% of all of Prospect Capital's investments as of March 31, 2025, were First Liens as opposed to 59.0% in the year ago quarter, which reflects to us a 6 1/2 percentage point shift in favor of First Lien investments.

In the March-quarter, Prospect Capital produced $196.1 million in gross originations which was the second-lowest amount in the last five quarters for the business development company. Originations, on a gross level, however, bounced upward 45% QoQ.

Since Prospect Capital also received a high number of repayments in 1Q25, of $191.7 million, the business development company produced very low net originations of $4.5 million. Since Prospect Capital's portfolio is not really growing, I don't anticipate a major change in income or coverage trends in the coming quarters.

Prospect Capital unfortunately exhibits a falling portfolio income trendline, thanks to high repayments as well as moderately higher non-accruals. In 1Q25, Prospect Capital experienced a 16% decrease in its total investment income and a 12% drop in its net investment income. This decrease in income was partially compensated for by Prospect Capital's dividend cut in the fourth quarter of last year.

Here are Prospect Capital's 5-quarter income and expense trends:

As far as Prospect Capital's actual credit quality is concerned, the business development company had a non-accrual ratio, as of March 31, 2025, of 0.6%, reflecting an increase of 0.2 percentage points QoQ.

Below I am showing-casing Prospect Capital's non-accrual development since the first calendar quarter of 2024. Though the non-accrual trendline is slightly sloped upward, non-accruals based on fair value are relatively low and are nothing to worry about, in my view.

Prospect Capital's 25% Dividend Cut Catalyzed Healthier Payout Metrics

Prospect Capital earned $0.19 per share in net investment income in the March-quarter, which implies a 71% dividend payout ratio. Prospect Capital cut its monthly dividend by 25% at the end of 2024 in order to realign it with lower net investment income expectations related to fewer investments in high-return strategies such as collateralized debt obligations.

A consequence of Prospect Capital adjusting its dividend lower in 4Q24 was that the BDC experienced relief in terms of the payout ratio. In the last twelve months, the BDC paid out 76% of its net investment, which is now one of the best ratios in the industry. The peer group average LTM payout ratio of the business development companies reflected in the chart below was 86%.

High Insider Ownership

I have repeatedly called out Prospect Capital for its dividend cuts as well as the BDC's falling net asset value, all of which have all not helped boost investor confidence in the business development company.

Compared to the average business development company in the industry, however, Prospect Capital's senior management has serious skin in the game and owns approximately 29% of the BDC's outstanding shares which I find to be redeeming quality and could suggest that the whopping 55% discount to net asset value is not fully warranted.

PSEC is Still Selling for an Industry-leading NAV Discount

Prospect Capital's stock is selling at a discount to net asset value, and not just a small one. As a matter of fact, Prospect Capital sells for the highest net asset value discount in the industry and BDC peer group thanks to the business development company having a falling net asset value track record. As a consequence, passive income investors don't have the highest degree of confidence in Prospect Capital's net asset value.

That being said, I still think that a more than 50% discount to net asset value is exaggerated, which in turn offers passive income investors a chance for a stock re-rating and attractive total returns on a risk-adjusted basis. I don't think that Prospect Capital with its history of sliding net asset value will ever really sell for net asset value, but a narrowing of the NAV discount is certainly in the cards in 2025, if the BDC doesn't mess up and maintains its present level of credit quality.

Taking into account that Prospect Capital is selling at a 55% discount to the last reported net asset value, which as of March 31, 2025, stood at $7.25 per share, it makes sense that the business development company is selling at the lowest Price-To-NII multiple as well: The BDC's stock is changing hands for a 4.3 times net investment income multiple, based on 1Q25 run-rate NII, which also reflects back to us a peer group leading NII yield of 23.1%. This valuation reflects a high margin of safety even for investors that are concerned about Prospect Capital's last dividend cut and concerning long-term NAV trend.

The average BDC in the BDC peer group is selling for a 19% discount to net asset value and a 7.2 times Price-To-NII Multiple, so I would assert that the margin of safety for PSEC here is indeed very high.

Prospect Capital is thus only a potential investment vehicle for passive income investors that understand that the BDC is not the first choice for many investors, certainly not for those that look for NAV stability.

Even BDCs with higher non-accruals, like Goldman Sachs BDC (GSBD) or Oaktree Specialty Lending Corp. (OCSL), are selling at much higher Price-To-NII multiples than Prospect Capital, which goes to show that dividend cuts and a sliding net asset value can do long-term damage to a BDC.

Why the Investment Thesis Might Be Bearish

Prospect Capital is very much a floating-rate business development company, which implies that the BDC could be impacted in a negative way by the central bank cutting short-term interest rates.

Inflation has moderated considerably in 2025 with recent inflation data pointing to a third consecutive month of cooling inflation. Moreover, Prospect Capital's non-accrual ratio is very low, which limits risks for the business development company.

My Takeaway

Prospect Capital has exhibited an expected decrease in its net investment income in 1Q25 due to a changing portfolio strategy and higher repayments, but the dividend payout ratio has improved. This is the result of the BDC taking a proactive approach by cutting down its cumulative dividend to $0.135 per share per quarter.

Prospect Capital still has pretty good credit quality, however, as shown by its low non-accrual ratio. The BDC's sudden change in investment approach last year as well as the dividend cut in 4Q24 have damaged investor confidence and sentiment.

That being said, the dividend is well-covered, which is why I think that the 55% discount to net asset value is widely out of touch with reality.

data.title: Meritage Homes: Rating Downgrade On Change In Demand Outlook
data.description:
Investment Overview

I wrote about Meritage Homes Corporation (NYSE:MTH) previously in Feb’25 with a buy rating, as I felt confident about the positive macro environment (back then) and that MTH continues to print solid results with strong orders and backlog conversion. My view has now shifted. With demand softening, incentives not being effective, and macro conditions worsening, I believe the risk-reward is no longer attractive. As such, I downgrade to a hold rating.

Demand Outlook Weakens Amidst Macroeconomic Headwinds

The housing market optimism that I had back in February no longer exists. In my opinion, the macroeconomic conditions have weakened dramatically, especially in recent months after the tariff announcements in April. MTH’s own commentary from the 1Q25 earnings also reflects this weakening backdrop. Management explicitly called out “volatile and elevated mortgage rates and fragile consumer sentiment due to increasing macroeconomic concerns.” While MTH is still noting that the spring selling season is healthy, I no longer have confidence that demand will remain strong.

The macro data points also paint a very negative demand outlook. Mortgage rates did dip in early 2025, but they are once again staying near 7%, and with the Fed holding off rate cuts, it basically wipes out any of my hopes for easing mortgage rates this year. Adding to that, consumer sentiment is also deteriorating. As a result, affordability for new homebuyers remains a headwind to housing demand recovery. What this suggests to me is that MTH will need to rely on offering deeper discounts just to keep demos coming in.

That pressure is clearly showing up in the numbers. MTH 1Q25 average selling price (ASP) on closings fell 6% y/y to $393,000, and ASP on new orders fell 1.6% to $402,000. I give management the credit for being honest here: that greater reliance on financing incentives, which rose sequentially from 4Q24, was the reason for this (basically validates my point above). But it doesn’t make it less worrisome. More importantly, management sees this trend continuing, with mentions that the use of pricing incentives will remain elevated for the near future.

Despite all this, demand softens. While 4Q24 saw a strong 14% y/y growth in net new orders, that flipped to a 2.9% decline in 1Q25 with a 2.6% y/y drop in closings. This is an important inflection. Unless macro conditions materially improve, I now believe MTH will see heavy earnings pressure in the coming quarters.

Lower Growth Potential due to Moderating Land Investment

One of the growth drivers I noted previously and was bullish on was the land acquisition strategy. Back in 4Q24, MTH spent a lot on land, but the latest data from 1Q25 suggests management has toned down on that front. Land acquisition and development spend came in at $465 million, and MTH only added ~2,200 net new lots, which is down sharply from the ~6,300 lots added in 1Q24. While MTH still controls an 84,200-lot portfolio as of 1Q25 (which management framed as a multi-year supply), the drop in new additions is still concerning.

My concern here is that if the rate of community openings (based on prior land purchases) starts to surpass the pace of new lot additions, it could lead to a natural slowdown in growth post-2025. The takeaway I got from management comments about land underwriting getting more challenged, sellers increasingly holding firm on pricing, and MTH walking away from ~1,600 lots in 1Q25 due to unfavorable terms suggests that it has become really difficult for MTH to acquire new lands at attractive rates. For what it's worth, it makes sense for management not to pursue when terms are not great, but it also means a reduction in near-term lot supply. If this trend persists, it could constrain MTH’s ability to scale at the same pace as previously.

Pressure on Profitability Beyond Gross Margins

One aspect that I did not touch deeply is the margin pressure MTH faces. I have touched briefly on the fact that gross margin will see pressure from elevated usage of incentives (in the risk section previously), but I am increasingly concerned about the pressure coming from increases in SG&A. In 1Q25, SG&A as a percentage of home closing revenue increased to 11.3%, up from 10.4% in 1Q24. The underlying reasons are the problem. The increase was due to (1) lower home closing volume, which resulted in limited fixed cost leverage, and (2) higher investments in tech and overhead related to MTH’s new divisions in the Gulf Coast and Huntsville.

For (1), that is very likely to remain a drag, since I don’t expect demand to see a strong recovery in the near term. For (2), while these expansions may be positive for long-term growth, they are a near-term drag on margin. If you combine the two, it just means that near-term margins will continue to face heavy pressure. On top of that, the tax line is another headwind. The effective tax rate jumped to 23.3% in 1Q25, up from 20.5% last year, and I think the new tax rate is here to stay since it was due to fewer homes qualifying for energy tax credits under the Inflation Reduction Act (FYI, new standards made it harder to qualify this year).

Valuation

I no longer think a multiple re-rating will happen for MTH. In fact, I think the discount to MTH’s historical trading average (8.3x forward PE) is justified against the weak demand outlook, lowered growth potential (fewer land lots), and margin pressure. For MTH stock to work (stock price to go up), the macro environment must change for the better, and this is something that is outside of MTH’s control. It can continue to press hard on offering incentives, but that comes at a cost (margin), and it is not working as well as it should (orders and closings are down y/y in 1Q25). If the macro situation continues to stay poor, the path forward for MTH earnings is likely down, and I don’t want to be a part of it.

Conclusion

I downgrade to a hold rating for MTH. There are simply too many variables working against the stock right now—high mortgage rates, weakening sentiment, incentives turning ineffective, and a moderation in land growth. The macro headwinds are just too much for the business (which is macro-sensitive) to offset with what it can do internally. Until affordability improves or rates come down materially, I think the stock will remain range-bound at best and pressured at worst.

data.title: National Presto: Magazine Shortage And Moat
data.description:
Introduction

It's been a while since I’ve written on Seeking Alpha, but I believe it's time to write again as market conditions and geopolitical realignments are creating very interesting and thought-provoking investments often overlooked.

Today, one of my favorite sleeper small-cap stocks recommendations is National Presto Industries (NYSE:NPK). The company is an under the radar American industrial with specialization in Defense munitions and, to a lesser extent, domestic appliances and safety systems. We believe the company is extremely well postured to weather current macro volatility as it is an American producer with an American supply chain with the ultimate American consumer–the US Government. Companies like NPK; with a vertically aligned value chain, are extremely well buttressed for future growth in the current macro environment.

Before we dig deeper, let's clarify our view. In April 2025, President Trump instated the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), this Act has implemented a 10% tariff on all imports, with specific higher rates for countries with significant trade deficits with the U.S., such as China, the European Union (EU), Canada, and Mexico. IEEPA has kicked off major insecurity among post-war allies, with follow-on court injunctions trying to halt the administration's movement. In retaliation, key trading partners have implemented anywhere from 34% from China on general American goods to a sweeping €21 billion net tariff tax from the EU. This instability, as we’ve noticed, has brought pressure on major international companies' equity valuations, especially those with larger international supply chain footprints. In short, it's been wildly disturbing for the market. With this disturbance, however, is a great opportunity.

While many market commentators are focused on the duration and seriousness of proposed tariffs; asking whether they’re just political posturing or a lasting structural shift; I believe investors are overlooking under the radar companies like NPK. Instead of buying $100+ billion market cap names on a 20% dip, long-term investors, deep value conviction investors will benefit from searching for opportunities like NPK. With a disciplined investment horizon of 3+ years, I believe NPK represents a strong base-case 1x return. In a bullish scenario, where geopolitical instability continues to deteriorate and domestic reindustrialization accelerates, NPK has the potential to become a 3–4x multi-bagger.

Magazine and Moat

Recently, Admiral Paparo of Indo-Pacific Command discussed the critical shortage the Department of Defense (DoD) has in munitions and the heightened concern a near pier conflict with China would see us unable to fight a conflict beyond a couple weeks. NPK is one of a handful of companies perfectly postured to fit this magazine depth concern as a tier one Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM). Via its subsidiary AMTEC, NPK provides 40mm ammunition rounds; 40mm rounds are high- and low-velocity tactical and training cartridges, including the advanced M918E2 TP-DNT round. Additionally, NPKs some other subsidiary–Spectra technologies create various warheads including, grenades, demolition devices as well as the DoD TOW® 2B missile and Small Diameter Bomb. All these products collectively equate to both 90% of the DoD 40mm supply as well as approximately 90% of NPK revenue. This is critical for the future success of the company and fundamental to our thesis.

What's Under the Hood

What we see here with the balance sheet is really assuring. Let's start with the pro’s. Cash reserves and marketable securities stay strong as the company holds $93.5 million in cash and equivalents, slightly down from $100.1 million, indicating ongoing liquidity without excessive burn. Additionally, and this is really important, the company holds zero long-term debt and working capital of $182.7 million, which indicates a conservative balance sheet and seemingly infinite foreseeable runway. In a capital intensive industry, these are salient points, especially when competitors are not as well aligned with our global market fragmentation thesis which favors self-sufficiency and being domestically anchored.

While this is all good and well, what's really interesting is the backlog to market cap ratio over the last ten years found in the annual 10Ks. See below for this:

Since year-end 2022, NPK market cap has been completely misaligned from company fundamentals. FY22 backlog orders were $477 million compared to $501 million market, juxtaposing this to FY25Q1 the market cap is $690 million, compared to an astounding $1.24 billion backlog, this effective backlog-to-market cap ratio went from 1.03x to 1.79x a dislocation which, I believe, is unwarranted and indicative of a mispricing. However, with good comes the bad and there are potential con’s associated, specifically, delayed revenue recognition, which is a legitimate concern. If there is any market uncertainty about execution, a discount of the backlog is warranted. Which brings to mind Boeing, which has suffered greatly from Boeing 737 Max 8’s recalls and delayed rollouts. Additionally, there are profitability concerns and operational capacity. As we know, increased backlog does not guarantee higher profits, and in fact, we’ve seen margin compression with reported gross margins of 24% in FY21 declining to 17% in FY23. However, since FY23 there has been stabilization at around 20%, which is a good thing. As for operational capacity, rapid backlog growth may bring in concerns for significant capital investment for additional facility and or labor expansion.

While there are no specific thoughts shared by CEO MaryJo Cohen, the recent forgoance of an extra dividend on top of the annual $1, has been looked at as a need to add working capital for potential facility expansion. This is both murky and disappointing, but, in my opinion, is also consistent with a small cap, low covered company, whose management is not tested by shareholder activism or public media attention, all of which allows the company to fly under the radar.

Forecasting

So far, the view is generally quite bullish on how NPK fits into the picture, but forecasting our projections should put some more meat on the bone. Keep in mind, we are in need of magazine expansion/munitions coupled with global market fragmentation. Here I will lay out three cases for NPKs future expansion.
