Full wartime economy scenario

Let me expand on this multi-year defense rearmament cycle. In the base case scenario, we see continued decoupling unfolding without a conflict-driven catalyst. Think if things continue as they are, NPK’s defense backlog execution should translate into steady revenue growth from $405 million in FY25 to approximately $478 million by FY27. This assumes stable margins, improving utilization, and maintenance of minimal debt. With this, we expect earnings per share (EPS) of $7, driving a modest valuation expansion to a 15x multiple. Under this case, NPK’s share price could move from $90 in 2025 to $117 by 2027, which would translate to about 13% Annualized Rate of Return (ARR). In a mid-case scenario, we would expect geopolitical flashpoints to intensify as well as substantial DoD initiatives– such as a sustained NATO buildup or heightened deterrence around Taiwan. This anticipates increased procurement velocity, benefiting domestic, vertically integrated suppliers like NPK. With backlog swelling past $1.5 billion and accelerated deliveries across AMTEC and Spectra, revenues could reach $538 million by 2027, with EPS nearing $8.50. This justifies a higher 17-18x multiple, potentially propelling the stock to $147 by 2027 and an ARR of 25%. In the most “optimistic”, aka horrific case (volatile) scenario a potential invasion by China on Taiwan– NPK as a tier one OEM would be thrust into wartime production, with its defense division operating at capacity under emergency DOD contracts. We would expect revenues exceeding $660 million and EPS surpassing $10, in turn, this would push the price well north of $200 on an anticipated 20-22x wartime premium multiple. We could speculate a share price around $300-400 (assuming no dilution and consistent execution of backlog). This would put ARR, using $350 as median, around 138%.

The key drivers of this model are Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) and revenue. For context, these formulas laid out below are assuming conservatives multiples conclusions at whatever case laid out. As discussed for the base case, we assume the company maintains its current pace of contract execution and industrial output, growing revenue modestly at a compound annual rate of just 3.5% from a 2024 base of roughly $4.6 billion. This yields a 2027 revenue projection of approximately $5.22 billion, using the formula: Revenue 2027 = Revenue 2024 × (1 + g)^n, where the Growth Rate (g) is 3.5% and the Forecast Horizon (n) is three years. For the mid-case, reflecting policy-driven industrial rearmament and supply chain onshoring, we modeled a 9% CAGR, resulting in projected revenue of $6.00 billion by 2027. In the optimistic case, where geopolitical decoupling drives full wartime procurement cycles, a 13% CAGR was applied, bringing estimated revenue to $6.75 billion. As for revenue, we forecasted estimate net income with the formula: Net Income = Revenue × Operating Margin × (1 - Tax Rate). For the base case, 8% operating margin and a 21% effective tax rate: $5.22 billion × 0.08 × (1 – 0.21) = $261 million in net income. The mid-case assumed a 9% operating margin on $6.00 billion of revenue, generating $317 million. The optimistic case applied a 10% margin to $6.75 billion in revenue, yielding $373 million. Each net income figure was divided by a share count of 37.3 million (anticipating no dilutive measures or share buyback) to derive EPS, and target prices were calculated using: Target Price = EPS × P/E. In conclusion, we layered this model on the scenario-driven valuations discussed in the prior paragraph, with conservative at each scenario to justify valuation. As you can see, across all these cases, we are expecting market performance substantially higher than the S&P 500 benchmark, which, I believe, is a rare asymmetric play that benefits from defense-driven globalization.

Counterpoints

While we may be extremely optimistic about how this investment fits into the general market conditions today, it does not come without legitimate counterpoints. First, the company’s current valuation already reflects a premium to peers in the small-cap industrials space. See below:

While most Aerospace/Defense (A/D) companies are unprofitable at this market cap classification, general industry comparables as we see are around 4x-6x P/E ratios. As stated, this would indicate that despite a very modest P/E compared to the S&P 500, NPK may actually be overvalued, especially when looking at its uneven earnings record and modest Return on Invested Capital (ROIC) outside the defense segment.

Second, is execution, execution, execution. As discussed, the backlog–while large, is not guaranteed revenue, even under the direction of seasoned CEO MaryJo Cohen. Any missteps could materially affect revenue realization and, worst case, lead to removal of tier one OEM status.

The third and least concern, is the housewares and safety segments, which together account for roughly 10% of revenue. These continue to underperform, posting low single-digit or even negative operating margins. These segments are effect drags on top and mid-line performance and, in our view, distract investors from recognizing real value here. Which is both a pro for entry base point but also a con since it could also distract from P/E multiple inflation.

The final concern, which is the biggest threat to the thesis, while admittedly would be good for geopolitical continuity, but bad for the thesis, is geopolitical cooling, i.e. normalization. While we still have 15 months to midterms and a touch under 3.5 years of the Trump administration left, the bullish A/D narrative could crumble if tariffs cool, or worse return to pre-Trump first term relations. This would materially realign away from defense spending and ultimately put pressure on the company as backlog new orders would collapse. While this is highly unlikely and quite frankly a tree-huggers view of the world, it would be detrimental.

Conclusion

In conclusion, NPKs recommendation is an asymmetric play weaved between a changing geopolitical environment of uncertainty, defense tailwinds and a Graham style small-cap dislocation. As a cash rich, debt free tier one OEM munitions supplier with DoD relevancy, the company is uniquely positioned to benefit from ongoing global fragmentation and U.S. reindustrialization. With counterpoint risks around execution, segment drag, and geopolitical de-escalation existing, we believe the asymmetric reward potential far outweighs these concerns, especially when taking NPK’s pristine balance sheet and entrenched defense footprint into account. In a market dominated by overvalued tech and fragile global supply chains, NPK is an excellent retirement account and growth prospect that retail investors can rest easy at night investing in.
